# Project Buildup History: Edge AI Runtime Evaluation

- Repository: `edge-ai-runtime-evaluation`
- Category: `advanced_system`

## 2025-02-10 - Day 1: Problem discovery start

- Task summary: Started this one by sitting down and trying to understand what Edge AI Runtime Evaluation should actually become. I did not rush into code yet. The main thing here was to write a simple problem statement for Edge AI Runtime Evaluation and the user it serves so the project had a decent base. I was still mostly figuring out the shape of it, so a lot of the day went into small notes and half clear ideas. There were a few moving parts here, so I kept simplifying the plan in my head before touching anything else.
- Deliverable: By the end of the day I had problem statement written down in a rough way, which was enough to stop guessing and move into the next step. I also left myself a few rough reminders for the next day so I would not have to rediscover the same context again.

## 2025-02-11 - Day 2: Research pass

- Task summary: Used the next day to keep the momentum going for Edge AI Runtime Evaluation. I was still piecing things together, so I focused on trying to collect examples, competitor references, and raw notes before choosing features and wrote down the stuff that looked important. At this stage nothing was fully locked, so I kept a rough list of things that looked useful and ignored the rest for now. The tricky bit was making the system sound realistic without pretending every edge case was solved.
- Deliverable: I wrapped up the day with research notes mostly in place. It was not perfect, but it was good enough to keep the project moving. There were still a couple of loose parts, but nothing serious enough to block the next step.
## 2025-02-12 - Day 3: Scope pass

- Task summary: By this point I had the rough direction, so the work became more practical. I spent most of the day trying to decide what belongs in version 1 and what can wait and make the whole thing feel less half baked. By now I had enough structure to keep going without staring at the screen too long, which honestly helped a lot. I spent part of the day just reducing confusion between components, names, and responsibilities.
- Deliverable: I wrapped up the day with feature scope mostly in place. It was not perfect, but it was good enough to keep the project moving. It was not super polished, just stable enough that I felt okay moving forward.
